---
title: SHAP analysis of Drosophila Development
author: Kris Sankaran
date: 2026-02-25
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    html-math-method: katex
execute:
    echo: true
    message: false
    warning: false
    cache: false
---

## Problem Formulation

1. Enhancers are stretches of the genome that coordinate the expression of many
downstream genes. Their status is determined by other genome elements, like
binding transcription factors and chromatin marks.

   ![](figures/enhancer.jpg){width=60%}

1. We care about them because they explain how genotype becomes phenotype.  This
is a complex question! So we study it in simple organisms, like fruit flies. We
know a lot about how enhancer (and other genome element) activity changes across
stages of embryo development.

   ![](figures/drosophila.gif){width=60%}

1. [@Basu2018] considered a more precise question: if we knew the state of the
genome at a given time of fruit fly embryo development, could we predict whether
a particular enhancer was active? Specifically, they work with genome-wide
measurements from blastoderm (stage 5) fruit fly embryos:

    - DNA occupancy for 23 transcription factors
    - Activity for 13 chromatin markers
    - Binary labels indicating enhancer activity for genomic regions

   Each observation is a genomic sequence with associated regulatory features.

1. They formulate this as a binary classification problem,

   - Enhancer status: $y \in \{0,1\}$
   - Predictors: $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_D)$ (transcription factor binding intensities and chromatin signals)
   - Model: $f(x_i) = \mathbb{P}(y_i = 1 \mid x_i)$

   We'll use SHAP to quantify each feature's contribution to $f(x_i)$.

## Setup

These are the packages we need for today. It looks like a lot, but the first
three are just to run a boosting model.

```{r}
#| label: setup
source("05-helpers.R")
library(mlr3)
library(mlr3learners)
library(mlr3tuning)
library(patchwork)
library(shapviz)
library(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_classic())
set.seed(20251227)
options(mlr3.store_backends = TRUE)
```

This block downloads and displays the data. We want to predict enhancer
activity, stored in the first column `y`.

```{r}
#| label: load-data
f <- tempfile()
download.file("https://zenodo.org/records/18371236/files/enhancer.Rdata?download=1", f)
load(f)

enhancer <- bind_cols(y = as.factor(Y), X)
enhancer
```

## Model Training

We'll train a boosting classifier using nearly the same code as the pockets
classification example from last week. As then, the first step is to define the
machine learning task and hyperparameter ranges.

```{r}
#| label: tune-setup
task <- as_task_classif(enhancer, target = "y", id = "enhancer")
learner <- lrn("classif.xgboost", nrounds = to_tune(100, 200), eta = to_tune(1e-3, 0.1), predict_type = "prob")

instance <- ti(
    task = task,
    learner = learner,
    resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3),
    measures = msr("classif.auc"),
    terminator = trm("none")
)
```

Next, we search over the number-of-trees and learning rate hyperparameters.

```{r}
#| label: tune
tuner <- tnr("grid_search", resolution = 5, batch_size = 5)
tuner$optimize(instance)
```

Let's refit the model with these tuned parameters.

```{r}
#| label: fit
learner$param_set$values <- instance$result_learner_param_vals
task <- as_task_classif(enhancer, target = "y", id = "enhancer")
learner$train(task)
```

The plot below compares the predicted probabilities to the true class. Most data
belong to class 0 (inactive enhancers), and these are correctly given low
probabilities. The active enhancers are rarer, and still sometimes difficult to
detect (peak on the left). But for many of these enhancers, we correctly have
higher probabilities of belonging to class 1.

```{r}
#| label: predictions
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 6
tibble(p_hat = learner$predict_newdata(enhancer)$prob[, 2], y = enhancer$y) |>
    ggplot() +
    geom_histogram(aes(p_hat, fill = y)) +
    facet_grid(y ~ ., scales = "free_y")
```

## Interpretation Setup

The $i^{th}$ row and $d^{th}$ column of `shp$S` below contains the attribution
$\varphi_d(x_i)$.

```{r}
#| label: shap-setup
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 4
X_mat <- as.matrix(X)
shp <- shapviz(learner$model, X_pred = X_mat)
shp$S[1:5, 1:8]
```

The efficiency axiom is satisfied, though on a log-odds scale (since we are
doing classification, not regression). While there are many definitions of local
feature importance, most do not provide this sum-to-prediction property.

```{r}
#| label: shap-efficiency
#| fig-height: 2.5
#| fig-width: 2.5
y_hat <- predict(learner$model, X_mat, outputmargin = TRUE)

tibble(y_hat = y_hat, sum_phi = rowSums(shp$S) + shp$baseline) |>
    ggplot() +
    geom_point(aes(y_hat, sum_phi)) +
    labs(
        x = "Model Prediction (Log-Odds)",
        y = "Sum of SHAP Attributions"
    )
```

### Variable-Level Pltos

We first visualize the features $d$ with the largest
$\left|\varphi_d(x_i)\right|$. We can see that across many samples, the
`wt_ZLD`, `input_c14c`, and `sna` stand out. For most samples, the attributions
for any given feature $d$ have a consistent sign, but there are tails that
switch the sign of the effect of that feature -- the model has learned that the
contribution of a feature to a prediction might be either positive or negative
depending on the particular feature value/context.

```{r}
#| label: importance
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 5
sv_importance(shp, kind = "bee")
```

We can look at the attributions plotted against the original feature value.
Whenever we have larger values of sna`, the attribution scores are negative.

```{r}
#| label: dependence
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 6
sv_dependence(shp, "sna1")
```

If you check the original data, large values of
[`sna1`](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SNAI1) are related to reduced enhancer
activity.

```{r}
```{r}
#| label: sna1-detail
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 6
tibble(p_hat = predict(learner$model, X_mat, outputmargin = TRUE), y = enhancer$y, x= enhancer$sna1) |>
    ggplot() +
    geom_point(aes(x, p_hat, col = y)) +
    labs(x = "Value of SNA1 Feature", y = "Log-Odds", col = "True Class")
```

### Sample-Level Plots

Alternatively, we can plot the most important features for specific samples of
interest. The waterfall plot below is read exactly like the example from the
conceptual handout. Each panel is a feature, horizontal bar is a feature, and by
following the starting/ending points across bars, we can track how individual
features lead to the final prediction.

```{r}
#| label: waterfall
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 6
map(seq_len(6), \(i) sv_waterfall(shp, max_display = 8, row = i)) |>
    reduce(`+`)
```

A downside of the waterfall plots is that they only make it possible to study a
few features at a time. We can study many more if we stack both features and
samples close to one another. It's important to sort the samples so that those
with similar attributions are placed with one another. The block below calls our
helper functions (in
[05-helpers.R](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/activities/05-helpers.R))
to perform this ordering.

```{r}
#| label: shap-bars
var_order <- order(abs(colSums(shp$S)), decreasing = TRUE)
important_vars <- colnames(shp$S)[var_order] |>
    head(11)

ix <- sample(1:nrow(enhancer), 500)
shap_bars <- shap_to_long(shp$S[ix, ], y_hat[ix], shp$baseline, important_vars) |>
    add_waterfall_offsets()
```

The way to read this plot is that each vertical slice is one sample, and its
colors are its attributions. We only show color for the 11 globally most
important features. The actual predictions are given by the "x" marks, and
features below the "x" are negative attributions and those above are positive
attributions. The use of this plot is that it highlights samples that, though
they may have similar predictions, have different reasons for getting their
predictions. For example `kr1` appears to be an important feature for flagging
one group of sequences (near the left) as enhancer, but it is not an important
feature for most other sequences.

```{r}
#| label: waterfall-custom
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-width: 8
ggplot(shap_bars) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
    geom_rect(aes(xmin = xpos - 0.45, xmax = xpos + 0.45, ymin = ymin, ymax = ymax, fill = name, col = name)) +
    geom_point(
        data = distinct(shap_bars, xpos, pred),
        aes(xpos, pred),
        size = 3,
        shape = "x"
    ) +
    labs(y = "Prediction relative to E[f(x)]", x = "Samples", fill = "Feature", col = "Feature") +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3", na.value = "#dcdcdc") +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3", na.value = "#dcdcdc") +
    theme(
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    )
```
